text:
Maternal mortality is the more recognised global health challenge but maternal morbidity has not been addressed sufficiently as an important aspect of women’s reproductive health in Kenya and the rest of sub-Saharan Africa. This paper attempted to bridge this knowledge gap by assessing the levels and factors associated with maternal morbidity using self reported obstetric complications that were derived from the 1998 KDHS data. This was a one off national survey in Kenya whose major aim was to generate information at the national and selected subcounty levels in respect of selected important factors relating to the health of women and children as well as provide reliable and relevant comparable data in Kenya at different time points. Issues associated with maternal morbidity measurement are briefly highlighted along with the various measurements with their pros and cons like the influence of recall and selection biases.

Our analysis showed that 53.6% of women in our cohort had at least one event reported in the childbirth morbidity module; the most common was prolonged labor, reported in 34.5% of women, followed by postpartum haemorrhage recorded in 23.3% of women. There was considerable between-province variation for all adverse maternal outcomes except postpartum haemorrhage, sepsis and eclampsia. From the multiple regression analysis, women reporting any morbidity were more likely to be multiparous, assisted in delivery by a doctor/midwife, from certain ethnic groups and residing in certain provinces. These data suggest that, in the context of the poor national vital registration process, that self-reported data using a population-based survey tool may represent the best available method of determining relative burden of maternal health problems, as well as assessing the health-care quality of the obstetric service provided to the population of sub-Saharan Africa.
explanation: The passage reads as a coherent, domain-specific summary with plausible, precise statistics and context typical of human-authored research. There are no obvious AI-generation artifacts or repetitive prompts, though AI could potentially mimic such scholarly text.
